content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
PARRINELLO, MUELLER & NAYLOR
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO                                591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                    SAN FRANCISCO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800                   MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941                650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814              TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                     SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                                                               TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800


                                          June 11, 1992


Deanne Stone, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 897
Sacramento, California  95812-0807

     Re:  Request for Informal Advice

Dear Deanne:

     This letter requests informal assistance under Government
Code section 83114(b).

     We represent a company which has received a call from a
local public official recommending that it host a reception for a
group of foreign business and political leaders visiting town.
We are concerned that the company may make a "gift" to the
official for the entire cost of the reception by paying for the
reception, because the official initiated the request and
provided the company with a list of the individuals to invite.

     The answer to our question turns on the interpretation of
your Advice Letter to Jack White (5/11/92), No. 92-277.  That
letter created a "protocol exception" to the definition of "gift"
in the Political Reform Act.  You concluded that Anaheim public
officials who asked Disneyland for tickets for "visiting
dignitaries" and certain other guests did not receive a gift from
Disney when Disney provided the dignitaries with the tickets.

     Does the "protocol exception" cover the situation in which a
public official asks a local company to sponsor a reception for a
delegation of foreign business and political leaders?  The
situation seems analogous to the provision of tickets to visiting
dignitaries, because the official himself will not receive any
benefit from the reception (other than his pro rata cost of the
refreshments, which he will count as a "gift" if he attends the
event and does not make a speech).  Like the Anaheim officials
who ask Disney to make Disneyland tickets available to visiting

_______________________

     1  The company believes that, as a matter of protocol, it
must ask the official who made the recommendation to attend the
event.

[The image appears to be a mostly blank or very faded document with minimal visible text. Only a few scattered dots or marks are visible across the page, but no clearly legible text can be reliably transcribed.]

Deanne Stone, Esq.
June 11, 1992
Page 2

dignitaries, the official is merely relaying a protocol request
that he receives due to his status as a local official.

Since neither foreign business or political leaders may
contribute to state or local campaigns, does it matter, for the
protocol exception, whether the visitors are business or
political leaders.

We appreciate your prompt response to this request.

Sincerely,

[signature]

James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr

6540.01
